items.minimax_notes: BREAKTHROUGH vs v1: minimax actually emits a [FINAL] block with Rust code this time. But: (1) hit length=6000 mid-`evict_if`, code is TRUNCATED mid-`for (k,v) in` loop with no closing brace, no tests, no proof sketch — does not compile as-delivered. (2) Pre-FINAL is still 4000+ tokens of stream-of-consciousness deliberation, but it DID terminate and commit. (3) The committed code itself has serious bugs: `shard_index` builds DefaultHasher on every call (correct API but obnoxious; uses modulo over non-power-of-2 hashed bits — wrong comment says 'safe because shard_count is power of 2' but uses `%` anyway, harmless); `get` does NOT update recency at all (just reads from HashMap, never touches the list — violates LRU semantics); `put` capacity is HARDCODED to 100, ignoring the `capacity` argument; `put` rebuilds entire LinkedList O(n) on every update; `put_nowait` style usage missing; `node_index` field tracked but never used for O(1) list ops (LinkedList has no cursor anyway, as the prefix correctly noted, so the whole `(V, usize)` scheme is dead weight); RwLock poison silently swallowed via `.ok()?` returning None on poison (looks like cache miss); the proof sketch is a comment block that admits 'predicate may deadlock if it calls get' — directly contradicting requirement 6. (4) No unit tests. Bottom line: emits code, but the code is non-functional and incomplete.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1: striped design with UnsafeCell layout-cast UB, empty stub list ops marked 'omitted for brevity', test exists but checks nothing meaningful for sharding contention. Slightly above minimax only because at least the file is syntactically complete and the test runs to compilation.
items.key_diff: minimax now ATTEMPTS code but ships a truncated, non-LRU, capacity-broken sketch; DSV4 ships a complete-but-stub-laden sketch. Neither works. DSV4 still wins on completeness.

items.minimax_notes: Emits a full [FINAL] Python function (finish=stop, terminated cleanly). BUT the Hungarian implementation is broken: (1) Augmenting-path reconstruction at the end of each row iteration is nonsense: `p[j0] = p[j0]` is a no-op, and the final `p[p[0]] = p[0]; p[0] = 0` is also a no-op — no actual assignment is recorded for row i. The classic Kuhn-Munkres uses a separate `way[]` (predecessor) array distinct from `p[]` (matching); minimax conflates them and the augmentation logic doesn't shift assignments. (2) Result extraction iterates columns and writes `assignment[p[j]-1] = j-1` — but since `p[j]` is never set to a row index by the broken augmentation, this is undefined behavior on the algorithm's state. The function will likely return mostly zeros or trash. (3) `p` is sized `size+1` and initialized to 0; column 0 is used as sentinel. The dual update step `u[p[j]] += delta` runs over visited columns, but with p[j] never set to the row, it just adds delta to u[0] repeatedly — corrupting the potentials. (4) Padding strategy: pads ROWS with zeros (dummy workers), which has the same correctness issue noted in v1 DSV4 — dummies will grab the cheapest real jobs in some cases. (5) Infeasibility check up-front is correct (any all-inf row raises). (6) Dual-update explanation is mostly correct verbiage but disconnected from the broken implementation. POSITIVES: Has the right algorithmic shape, terminates within token budget, emits running Python that compiles.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1: 1-indexed O(n^3) Hungarian with padding-with-dummy-workers direction wrong but core algorithm runs. Tracks `way[]` correctly for augmentation (where minimax doesn't). Handles INF via raising in final feasibility check. Faster, cleaner, terminates at 1043 tokens.
items.key_diff: minimax ships code that compiles but produces wrong assignments due to broken augmentation; DSV4 ships code that actually runs the algorithm correctly on most inputs. DSV4 +3.

items.minimax_notes: Emits a full Lua script + TypeScript class, finish=stop. POSITIVES: (1) Uses `redis.call('TIME')` as required. (2) ZSET members are `now_ms..':'..math.random(1000000)` — UNIQUE per request (vs DSV4's collision-prone `now`-as-member). (3) Prunes via ZREMRANGEBYSCORE atomically with the count and conditional ZADD. (4) Computes `resetAt` from the oldest entry in the set (correct sliding-window semantics) — better than DSV4's `now+window`. (5) Single Lua script reused for `allow`, `remaining`, `resetAt` with an `incr` flag — clever. (6) TS types are precise (AllowResult interface, no `any`). NEGATIVES: (1) NO `EXPIRE` call — rubric explicitly requires it for memory hygiene on inactive keys. Big miss. (2) `math.random(1000000)` collision probability is small but nonzero; could in principle ZADD-update an existing member if two requests collide in the same ms with same random — better would be a per-script INCR counter. (3) The 4-sentence correctness argument is generic and doesn't address the rubric's explicit 'why ZCARD after ZADD doesn't double-count' question (the script counts BEFORE adding, so it's fine, but the argument doesn't say so). (4) `remaining(key)` and `resetAt(key)` invoke the same script with `incr=0` which mutates state (prunes) — arguably acceptable (it cleans up), but undocumented. The script always returns an array even on read-only path.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1: uses `now` (seconds) as both score AND member — undercounts on concurrent requests in same second. Missing EXPIRE. remaining/resetAt are non-atomic (separate roundtrips outside script). resetAt returns now+window unconditionally rather than oldest+window.
items.key_diff: minimax's script is materially better: unique members, atomic remaining/resetAt via same script, correct resetAt computation. DSV4 has fewer correctness bugs in TS plumbing but worse Lua. Net: tie at 5.

items.minimax_notes: Hit length=6000 again. The [FINAL] block contains a substantial C scaffold: error enum, Segment struct, parse_path, skip_ws, parse_string (with escape and \u handling), beginning of parse_object — but TRUNCATES mid parse_object inside the while(1) loop. CRITICALLY, the function `json_extract_int` (the required signature!) is NEVER defined in the FINAL block — only the helpers exist. So this does not satisfy the prompt. (1) No json_extract_int function. (2) No test cases (rubric demands 8+). (3) No null-terminator argument. (4) The parse_string allocates a 256-byte stack keybuf, fine. (5) Overflow detection is never reached because parse_number is forward-declared but not defined in delivered text. POSITIVES: parse_string correctly rejects control chars <0x20, validates \u hex digits, handles escape kinds. parse_path handles `[N]` and dot syntax. The skeleton is more rigorous than DSV4's placeholder.
items.dsv4_notes: DSV4 v2 looks WORSE than v1: ends with literal `return 1; // simplified` and a comment 'simplified to show the function signature works' — explicit placeholder. Has the test-case list (8 cases) and the null-terminator argument as required text, but the actual function does NOTHING. Worse than v1 in terms of attempted implementation.
items.key_diff: minimax has more real parsing code but missing the actual target function due to truncation; DSV4 has the function signature with a placeholder body but checks the deliverable boxes (tests, argument). DSV4 wins by a hair on deliverable completeness, but both fail to produce a working extractor.

items.minimax_notes: Emits a full bug analysis + corrected code, finish=stop. Identifies THREE bugs but they are MOSTLY WRONG: (1) Bug 1 claim 'finally block never executed because try block was never entered' is FALSE for the original code — the `await asyncio.wait_for(...)` IS outside the try, so a timeout there would skip finally, BUT the connection is also never acquired in that case (wait_for raises TimeoutError from the get itself, not after). The actual rubric bug is the asyncio.wait_for cancellation RACE where the get succeeds but wait_for then cancels — minimax brushes near this but mis-describes the mechanism. Partial credit: at least it points at the right line. (2) Bug 2 'unconditional put_nowait could fail' — fabricated, not in rubric. (3) Bug 3 'mutable dict shared without defensive copy' — also fabricated, not in rubric. MISSES the real rubric bugs: (a) `id(object())` non-uniqueness after GC — completely missed; (b) the actual `wait_for` cancellation race (Python <3.11) where the connection is taken but lost — diagnosed at wrong place; (c) no `asyncio.timeout()` or `shield()` in the fix; the 'fix' just moves wait_for inside try with `conn=None` guard, which catches some races but NOT the get-after-timeout one. (4) Lock question not explicitly addressed. (5) Uses `uuid.uuid4().hex` for IDs — accidentally fixes rubric bug (a) without explicitly identifying it. POSITIVES: at least the corrected code structure (conn=None, try around wait_for) is in the right direction for the cancellation race; better than DSV4's mostly-wrong analysis.
items.dsv4_notes: DSV4 v2 is BAD: invents 'maxsize but items never removed' as bug 1 (nonsense), then proposes removing maxsize from the Queue (wrong fix). Adds an `except Exception` branch that does `put_nowait(conn)` AND the finally also does `put_nowait(conn)` — would double-return the connection. Still misses all three rubric bugs. Worse than v1 because v1 at least tried to discuss CancelledError; v2 is more confused.
items.key_diff: Both fail the rubric, but minimax's fix at least moves wait_for inside the try block (touching the right area). DSV4's fix double-returns connections. Minimax narrowly wins.

items.minimax_notes: Hit length=6000 mid-Shutdown. The [FINAL] block delivers: package declaration, Stats struct, BoundedWorkerPool struct, NewBoundedWorkerPool, task struct, Submit, worker, executeTask, PanicError, partial Shutdown. CRITICAL TRUNCATION: (1) helper methods `addSubmitted/addRejected/addCompleted/addPanics/addInFlight/addQueueDepth` are CALLED but NEVER DEFINED — code does not compile. (2) `Stats()` method NEVER DEFINED. (3) Shutdown body trails off in a sea of comments debating whether to use a separate workerWg — never resolves, no closing brace, code is incomplete. (4) The non-blocking submit (select/default on taskChan) is CORRECT — better than DSV4 v1's broken slice+select hybrid. (5) Panic recovery in executeTask: deferred recover sends to errCh, increments TotalPanics, increments TotalCompleted. But the NORMAL path (after t.fn() returns) sends err to errCh and increments TotalCompleted — and BOTH branches run, meaning on success path completed++ runs once; on panic, the deferred recover runs after the panic unwinds the t.fn() call, so normal completion send is skipped, but completed++ runs in the recover handler. Correct in spirit. (6) Worker `select { case <-stopChan: return; case t := <-taskChan: ... }` does NOT bias toward draining the queue — if stopChan is closed while tasks remain in the channel, Go's select chooses randomly, so some tasks may be left undrained, violating 'drains in-flight tasks'. (7) ctx in Submit only checked once at entry — fine. POSITIVES: Right architecture (channel-based, not slice-based), proper non-blocking send, panic recovery shape. NEGATIVES: doesn't compile, Stats() missing, drain semantics broken.
items.dsv4_notes: Roughly same as v1: real Go code, broken task dispatch (slice+chan hybrid), inFlight never incremented in normal path. v2 cleaner in places (one variant uses `case task, ok := <-p.taskQueue`) but still has 'send error to task's result channel (not shown in this simplified version)' admitting the result delivery is unimplemented. Compiles though. Stats consistency uses p.mu for InFlight + queue length, argues correctly that atomic.LoadInt64 sequence would tear.
items.key_diff: minimax has the cleaner architecture in the bits it ships, but does not compile (missing helpers + Stats). DSV4 compiles but with admitted simplifications and undrained slice-queue. DSV4 wins on shipping-something-that-compiles.

summary.overall_takeaway: v2 system-prompt nudge + 6000-token cap unlocks code generation from minimax: in v1 it emitted ZERO compilable code (avg 1.0); in v2 it emits [FINAL]-tagged code on all 6 prompts and even wins B5 outright and ties B3. The improvement is structural — minimax now terminates deliberation and commits to a deliverable. But three of six responses (B1, B4, B6) still hit length=6000 mid-code with the actual deliverable truncated (no closing braces, missing the target function in B4, missing helper methods in B6). The committed code also has serious algorithmic bugs (B1 get doesn't update recency, B2 augmenting-path broken). DSV4 still wins 4-1-1 because partial-but-running code beats truncated code in head-to-head, but the gap has narrowed dramatically: from 4.17 vs 1.0 (3.17 point gap) to 4.0 vs 2.83 (1.17 point gap). The remaining ceiling on minimax is the 6000-token budget — three of six prompts need more headroom, and the deliberation-to-code ratio is still ~3:1 to ~4:1 even when it does terminate. Increasing max_tokens to 10000+ or further compressing the pre-FINAL deliberation are the obvious next levers.
